What's going on? I don't know if you heard those, had those Kirkland questbars, like the fake questbars. I think they're way better. Okay. So, how great is my wife? Right? She's unbelievable. Like, I saw that presentation, I was like, crap. I was like, I see now I get to organize all the speakers so I never had to talk directly after her or if I did have to talk, I was like with her so I'm like, I'm going to get some of this credit. But now I just have to like make something that hopefully, well, it'll pale in comparison but hopefully is useful. For those of you who, right now, because, so, the reason that going from zero to a million dollars a year is something that a lot of businesses achieve in revenue. But going from a million to three or three to ten, even a lot of like the sales and marketing gurus that you see all over the place, the reason a lot of them can't actually make that transition is not because of a lack of skills and sales and marketing but it's because of everything that Layla just talked about. Is that a certain point you need to have an infrastructure and a team? And I was talking with Ben Krimis about this a second ago. But anyone here, you guys have heard of Dan Kennedy, right? Dan is the man. Dan is dear, near and dear to my heart. He will probably be a person that I just give a big, big bag of money one day and just say, hey, thanks. But the reason that he was never actually able to build his business past, I don't think he ever got to 10 million a year in revenue despite being the genius marketer in sales that he was because he just hates people. And I mean, I don't think that for those of you who have met Dan, he's not like a, I mean, he's not the biggest person. Anyways, you can read his book about how to manage people and you can kind of see that. And the reason that we've been able to grow is because of all the stuff that Layla said. And I'm like imploring you to not do that because the reason that we get the credit that we have for the marketing sales we do is because of all that. So my first presentation was about knowing your critical number, knowing what number, so that you have absolute clarity in terms of what you need to do and what you and your team need to strive towards so that you can cut away all the rest of the distraction. The second presentation was the things that you can do tomorrow to increase the profitability of your business so that that number can actually come faster towards you, even at your current gym. This presentation is not about profit, but how you multiply profit. And it made the most sense coming after Layla's presentation. And so I hope you enjoy it and I hope you take all the good use away. So I already covered that. Okay. Oh, wow. I really think the same way when I make these presentations. So what I'm going to talk to you about is the unconventional way that we approach business and how this one kind of central concept has helped us become the biggest player in this space by a long shot in under a year. And this has been a pretty uninterrupted space for the most part. It's had one incumbent for a very long time and how we were able to massively disrupt it using this concept and how you can model this approach to make your gym more profitable and enjoy every day a lot more. And I'll wrap it up with tactics at the end because it's really hard for me not to. But I hope you guys enjoy this because I think this has been, oops, so is that okay? Oh, dang it. Is that okay? Okay. So just follow along with me as I walk you through one of the main mindset shifts that's been a profound impact on our bottom line. And it's a concept that I'm calling weapons grade compassion. And I'm referencing how you think about your employees and your customers. And when you can kind of enter this headspace and truly care, you can unlock a level of creativity and execution that you never knew existed before that. Does anyone know what I'm talking about or feel like they know what I'm talking about? Well, first of all, you've experienced this before as an entrepreneur, where you get into a space of absolute selflessness. And maybe it's like, has anyone here had a one year anniversary event at their gym? You know, after everything's done and everyone leaves and the gym's quiet, you're like, wow, you get this feeling. And I feel like it's this moment where you kind of get outside of yourself. And you just think about everything that's happened. And then you kind of step outside of time. And you're like, what's happened before? Where are we going? And it's just this true appreciation of the moment and for all those people that you serve. And we get out of the, what's our churn? What's our retention rate? How much will handwritten cards decrease my churn? How can I increase my lifetime value? When do we want to do our town hall meetings so we can tell them that we're going to be adding $12 a week to their EFT? So you get out of all that and just kind of appreciate. And if you've been in one of those states and then all of a sudden you come to this conclusion that's super crystal clear, anyone? No one? Cool. Okay. So that's what Jim Lauch is doing. And in my opinion, and this is what's been, I think, central to the reason that Jim Lauch has been so successful, is that it's taking full responsibility for the other person's state and taking action as a result of that from the perspective of someone who can just choose to help because you can, just because you can. And so it's that perspective from both how you manage your employees and then also how you manage your customers. If you can approach it from that perspective, like I said, of weapons-grade compassion, it's just amazing what can actually happen. And I'll get nowhere, there will be more tactics, but I just want to kind of overview. Because whenever you help other people, you're by default operating from a place of abundance. So who here is like, man, I really struggle with having a scarcity mindset. I still worry about paying bills sometimes. Sometimes another gym is going to open up in my area and then I start worrying. Right? I know. Only one person struggles with that, which is fantastic. No one else? Am I good enough? No one's ever asked that question in the self. That's good. But the cool thing is that when you really have this weapons-grade compassion, you really start looking at other people and thinking, how can I serve these people? How can I serve these people? You default, operate from a place of abundance, which I believe is the entrepreneur's super secret power. Because everyone sees this on Instagram all the time. Because when we think about us, we make really dumb stuff. We make dumb decisions about the business. We don't think about people. We don't think about consequences. We make terrible hiring decisions. We make terrible management decisions. And ultimately, we isolate ourselves because of our ego and our pride. And it's hard because as you succeed, more people will tell you that you're successful. And it feels good to tell people that we're doing well. But everyone hates it when they hear it and doesn't like you. I mean, we've been able to ride this ride firsthand and experience it even family members and other things like that that just get weird about what's happening. You know what I mean? Like, when they see the award that we have with $10 million on it, they're like, what? You're my nephew. And I'll still tell you how to run a business. I'm like, all right. Thanks, Uncle Daryush. Because we're Persian and that's how we name our children and stuff. So when entrepreneurs aren't thinking of themselves, like when we're not thinking about us, I think instead of making really dumb stuff like this, we make really amazing stuff like this. And I think the single reason that Elon Musk, for example, is so motivated and is going to like he literally is operating from place of he thinks he's deluded himself in a good way because we all delude ourselves. And to believe he's like, I'm saving the human race. And his entire company because he has three companies, but they actually operate under one umbrella because he's trying to get mankind to space. Like that's his mission. So he's like, we need to have electricity on space. We have to have sick batteries. We need to have solar so that we can survive off the sun. And we need to have homes and cars that can operate off of this electric base. So like the whole thing, despite being three entities, is one concept that he's been driving towards because and he doesn't need the money. And when you can operate from that space, crazy stuff happens. And I think that people can feel if you're operating from that space. I think that your customers can feel that. And I hope I'm not coming off like I'm shooting my own horn. But a lot of times the feedback that we get, or at least the feedback that I see online between Jim Onaris is like, man, Alex and they're like, they're so real. They're so like authentic and all that stuff. And I think it's genuine because like, you're like, you're one of my people. Like you guys are my homies. And it's like the biggest heartbreak of these events for me is I want to sit down one on one with everyone for four hours and be like, OK, tell me all your numbers. Where are you at? Where's your team? Where are you feeling pressure? Where can you move around? And it's a physical impossibility and it kills me. But I think that people can feel that. And it was something that I had to learn over a really long period of time and I've been blessed enough to be able to serve my people, you guys, to have learned this lesson, unfortunately for me, after I exited my gyms. Because I ran my gyms like a hardcore business and I really didn't resonate with people who needed to lose weight because that was so never my issue. I was like 15 with a six pack and like, it's never really struggled with eating. I was like, I can eat pop darts and look great. I'm being honest. And so it was something that never really resonated with me, but only in seeing how much growth we've had as a company did other things that other mentors and people who made much more than me about how you have to love your customers and be obsessed with your customers and think about their outcomes constantly. Once I only obsessed about you guys because you guys were like an extension of myself, that's what it feels like, did all these crazy things start happening because I don't know if anyone here is taking their clients' results and their outcomes personally to the extent that we take and I'm not, I promise I'm not trying to take this as like a shooting horn thing that's not my intention. Like, you know what I mean? Like that's how they think about it. But it's because he's taking full responsibility for the outcome of his customers, which is delighting them. Right. So what they come in for, there's something you write down. What people come in for is what they give you money to fix. What they keep coming back for is everything else that you give them they did not pay for, that you did not promise them. So if people sign up for gym launch, right? And they, and we say, Hey, we're going to help you fill your gym. And then we help them fill their gym. But while they have it, they get plugged into our community. They see they get exposed to some of the leaders in this community and they start feeling like they're personally growing. They see the advancements in the updates that are happening on a regular basis. They get on the phone with support and supports like totally blows them away and totally cares about them and stays longer and schedule a second call to follow up with them just to see how they're doing. Right. They're like, Holy crap. This is unbelievable. This is like, they gave us money to fill their gym, but they stay and keep buying y'all because hopefully you got something else that you, that we didn't originally promise that it was like above and beyond. And that's where the abundance comes in. It's abundant exchange. Like you gave us money and tip, like normal exchanges. You gave me money for a shirt. I'll give you a shirt. Abundance changes. You give me money for a shirt. I give you a shirt and I give you a photo shoot in the shirt. Right. And then like we walk you out on stage with like music behind you and then you're like, man, this is like, I love this shirt. Right. Is it the shirt or the experience and it's both. Right. That's called abundant exchange. And the thing is, is that it takes work to shift your mindset for yourself first and then to do this in your staff. We market internal challenges to our employees, right? You're retaining your customers, you're retaining your employees, you want to ascend your customers, you want to ascend your employees. It's totally parallel. It was this huge breakthrough that we had in terms of knowing how to run our business and that it's not just a dual marketing calendar, which is what I originally taught, which is like you have all of your external, you have your prospects and then your customers. It's actually a quad marketing calendar. You have your potential new hires that are in the marketplace. You have your current new hire, your current employees that you're marketing to have culture within your business, which is the same as having retention to your customers and then prospects. And as entrepreneurs, we have to operate from that. Spend it to 100% responsibility because we're the, because we're the only ones who can control ourselves, right? And so the hard thing about this is that it's making the first shift of admitting fault that's the hardest. So some of you guys, even some of the questions, I don't know if some of you guys like notice it is when, like, when I'm listening to someone asking questions, we're really not listening to what your questions are. We're trying to figure out like where you're operating from. So if someone's like, I just hired this person and they're not doing this and they said they were going to do this and they didn't do this and they didn't do this, like I didn't hear me once and I didn't hear about what like, what do I need to do to be a better leader so I can motivate this person. I always come from the perspective of, I think that God, whatever your God is, would be the perfect leader and manager and he could manage anyone, right? And so it's always my deficit if someone has to leave our business is because I don't have the skill set to manage them and that's been my deficiency, not theirs. And if you can think about it like that, you'll grow a lot more. Oh, here. I'm not playing that. Slides. And making that first shift is the hardest, especially if you operate from like a victim mindset. Some of you guys do. I know. I see your comments. I see your posts. Some of you guys do. You just need to shake it because it's like, shirt feels good. I like to wallow and then other people commiserate with you. You'll make more money. Promise. And it's still miserable. It's not fun either. And so one of the biggest things here to shift this is to give when you don't feel like you have anything at all. And that's where that abundant exchange comes in. That's when you're like, man, I feel like we don't have anything else left to give and that's the best moment to give. Because that's when like, Myron says, you break and then you stretch. But that first give is what unlocks some of our greatest inner strength, which is what grows us. And so when you can operate from that headspace, you've never done enough. This is actually a big point. I'm happy the next couple of slides. Very proud of the next couple of slides. But you never think like, I give them more than enough. Some people are like, I can't believe they want more. I give them so much already. Even if I operated that way for our business, who needs releases anymore? Whatever. It's great. But that's just our ego's talking. And as long as they are humans, as long as everyone here is human and you want to help them, so what if you give them more than you're charging for? Like who here came like, loves to help people. That's like, why you became an entrepreneur? You want to help people totally. So who cares if you help them more? Great. Then you just put money into the like, I'm a good person bank. You know what I mean? Like I feel good about myself bank because it does feel good to help other people. And to just not like, nah, I'm not charging anything for. I just want to love on people. And so I do have to have a special note here. Do all that, but make sure that your business model makes sense. It doesn't mean that you need to have $50 a month personal training. That's not my point. The point like, use discernment. As long as your model makes sense and you can have a profit and you can live off of it. So what if you spoil your people or your events? You're like, I spent a little bit too much of my time, but it was so worth it. Like, awesome. That's life. You know what I mean? Like we're living it. We're enjoying it. You're soaking it in. And that's the whole game. So here's an example that I thought was relevant. I'm like, would it be bad if you gave your spouse a back rub and they didn't give you one back? No, you just like put money in the bank. I'll get that later. I don't know what's coming. I don't know what's coming. You know what I'm saying? Thank you for the one person who gives back rubs. Not expecting anything. But this is a reality that you have to face, especially as business owners, is that no one will ever give you the same amount of credit that you believe you deserve ever. So just get over it. It's never going to happen. You know what I mean? We released Leagent 2.0, which put a ton of work into. Mel crushed it and worked with 16 different gem owners. We spent 40 grand over just a certain amount of time with 373 ads to do it. And right after we released it, someone was like, I was really disappointed with the module. I got a message. I was like, okay. Like, cool. You know what I mean? Like, it happens. Right? And I also think, and my math side kicks in whenever I get to stress now, and something that's, I think it's a good protective mechanism for me. I got one complaint. I got 450 gems. I was like, so I'm going to like 0.25% complaint. I'm like, okay, I'm good. I was like, if I keep it under less than 5%, I was like, I'm fine. So, which would be like 25, which would seem like a catastrophe, right? If that were to actually happen. But that's what kicks in for me. That's my defense mechanism. But just accept that the world isn't fair, right? And choose to live in the world where you control the rules because of how much you give, right? You get to control the rules when you get to give more than you're asking for, right? Does that make sense? Okay. So, here's how this operating manifesto affects our bottom line. And I think this is where it's cool. Is that for us, this level of love, dedication, obsession manifests in two ways that are measurable. In my opinion, they're these two things. First time value, retention of a customer, and then premium pricing. Which is what goes back to like, hey, if you're giving more than you're charging for, you should still charge a decent amount. And you guys know better than me, but I think it comes down to never settling and continuing to provide value, right? And the key is to truly, is loving your customer and loving your bit or for the few of you who are like, I really just don't care about weight loss anymore. Be honest with yourself. If you don't, then love your business so much that you love your customer indirectly. I love you guys so much, and it's true that I'm thinking about ways to solve weight loss problems now that I never did as a gym owner because I know that it's going to help you guys out. Which is weird for me to think about, right? So if you can channel that into like, I love this business, I love being a business owner, I love owning a business, and using that as your fuel to make an amazing product. I have a personal for inner friend of mine who opened a cookie store, only for business. He does, he's like, I don't care about cookies. The business model makes sense. But he obsesses about making his cookies great because he likes to be good at what he does. That makes sense? So if you're not operating from this customer love standpoint, you can still get there through the pathway mentally of like, I love this growth, I love being a business owner, and I love this business, and I want it to be amazing because I'm associated with it, right? And he's around, like, man, I'm going to cover all that. And so I think customers feel that occasion towards growth and solving their problems, and when it's there, they stay and grow, right? And when it's not there, people leave because they feel like they've gotten everything. Who here left a mastermind because they had gotten everything? I know how many of you did. And they don't think that there's anything that they're going to grow anymore. And if you feel like you've already given them enough, then they're right. They have gotten everything, and then they will leave. Does that go full circle? So like, you don't want to give anymore, and then they're like, I think I've gotten everything that I'm going to get, and then they leave. And so the big secret to recurring revenue is that it's not recurring. That's the big secret. People are like, how have you built this high ticket recurring revenue machine, and you don't have one-on-one coaching calls with everyone? I was like, because one-on-one coaching calls are therapy, A, which I don't know. Most times it's personal. It's a U-thing. But second, because we have to keep giving. And so I believe that, so within the internet marketing space, people will poo-poo the launch model, which is basically like, you have an audience. You create a product, launch, it sells out, card ends on midnight, and then it's done, and then you don't make any money for another six months, you make another product, and you do the whole thing. And that's the cycle, right? And they're like, it's horrible because times in between you have no money coming in, and you don't know what you're going to do. The reality is that when you're building a recurring-based revenue model, it is a launch model. You're just doing it every month. That's all. That's it. That's a huge nugget. I'm just letting you know. And so if you're looking at your recurring revenue model, you have to think, okay, what can I give this month? What can I give this month? What can I give this month? What can I do to make these people feel like they're still growing and that organization is growing so that they can be a part of something that's growing? Movements grow. If it's not growing, it's not a movement. And you guys all want to start local movements within your communities, right? So it has to grow, which means you have to give, and you have to give more than you're charging for. So I think this has been our unstated reason for success. Really care. And we really want everyone to grow and make more money more easily. We really actually do. I can't tell you how many times, but I'll have marketers who are like, yeah, yeah, yeah. But what is it really? We really do that. And so the key for us is how do we keep forcing ourselves to grow and by extensions continue to innovate and learn and pass those learnings down? And so I wonder, do you guys feel like you're doing this with your facilities? Do you feel like you're actively thinking like, how can I get more uncomfortable? How can I grow? How can I grow as a leader? What things do I need to pass on to my customers? Where are they getting stuck more? Like, consciously just thinking like, never in this space of like, I give them more than enough as this. Because as soon as you do that, you're screwed because someone else is willing to give more than you. And they'll leave to where it's growing. And so I knew that if I think back to, if I were running my gyms again and I had Alayla, there'd be a lot of things how to run differently. Because if you're raising your hand again, if you're doing like 40 or 50 every month, because that's what we do in legacy, so most gyms aren't close to 750 EFTs. So how are we doing it? Because of our retention. And so I know that there are other gurus who have courses and masterminds and whatever, but many of them have to keep acquiring customers to plug their holes. And I think that we acquire customers a little bit faster, for sure, but we mostly grow because we don't lose any. And so there's this revolving door and all, I see the marketplace, like hundreds of revolving doors and then one piece of glue that all of them eventually hit and then the thing just snowballs. The other doors are still spinning. It's just that one of them just keeps accumulating everyone. Money works the same way, too. A lot of people are just revolving doors with money. They get it, they give it, they get it, they give it, and then one guy sucks all of it. So be that guy. Okay. And to show the true power of this, here's a little example of over-delivering and the impact on your bank account. So the reason I call this profit multiplication is because weapons-grade compassion will multiply how much profit you take home. Profit, not revenue, takes revenue too, but profit. So gym one, you're doing 30 EFTs a month, 10% attrition. Your max is gonna be 300 EFTs. And it's gonna take you two years to get there at that rate, because when you're at month 15, you're like, it's like grows by five and grows by three. Like it's really small as it gets towards the asymptote. Don't worry about it. That's okay, math. But gym number two, let's say Nicole's gym, she's just in front size here. She's doing 30 EFTs per month, same as gym number one, but they just have a 3% attrition rate. 10% to 3%. Doesn't seem like that big of a difference, right? Maximum, 1000 EFTs. Big difference. Seems like small difference. Massive difference, right? And so a 7% decrease in turnover is a 300% increase in revenue. And that's also because 7% is actually 300% decrease from 10% to three. But that's why it's a multiplier. So if you can unlock this, it seems like it's a soft, gushy subject that I talked about this whole time. But if you can start operating from that headspace, you can start increasing the lifetime value of your customers and ultimately make a significantly more profitable gym. It also helps you sleep better at night. Because you know that no one's gonna leave, right? So despite me being kind of an A-hole during my gym days and having people run through the lobby because they didn't wanna talk to me because I was gonna take money out of their pocket, despite that, I still, the whole time, only had one trainer leave all of my locations and he took five customers. And I was never there. I mean, never. I hated being at the gym by the end. Like, just because of me being an idiot. So, quick example number two. So if everyone else in your area is charging $99 a month for a large group and they have 120, right? That's 12,000. And let's say they're taking home 6,000 a month, right? Oh, sorry, $2,000 a month, excuse me. So 10 is, and they're probably training sessions and doing some other stuff, right? So that's where they're at. This is where a lot of people were when they came into this. The average gym owner makes $500 a week. Right? Some of you guys know that. And then you decide to charge $200 a month for a large group and they have 100 people in day-home, $6,000 a month. Oh, I did the math right, it should have been 120. But even with this, you take home $6,000 a month instead of $2,000 a month. And so because you're able to charge higher prices, you're able to not take home just twice as much for two or three times as much money, right? This is actually from less customers too. So I should have done apples to apples, so that's just my goof up. But it would have been like 7,500, so like three and a half. Right? So the two areas that this will most benefit you is how much you can price, how much margin you leave in there for yourself and how long people will continue to pay that margin over and over again. And also creates this moat where no one will ever leave you. Okay. And so over-delivering gives you the ability to charge higher while retaining people, which is the virtuous cycle of price. So this is the second of the many infographics that will be in the Infographic Book. I know, get yours now. I thought they were kind of cool-handed. Is that cool? Is that like cooler? I don't know. I thought it was cooler, rather than like just like another PowerPoint thing. But basically, I'm just gonna overview this because some of you need to hear it again. And for those of you who are new, this may be the first time you've heard it. For those of you who are old, I like hearing it myself. But there's a virtuous versus vicious cycle of pricing. And when I tell you guys to raise your prices, I want you to do two extra prices and 10 extra service. So at the end of the day, the recent people stayed because they value discrepancy. So I wanna always be the most expensive in the marketplace, but I want the fact that I'm the most expensive in the marketplace to give me the resources to do things like this that no one else can do, and then still make us more money. So it works both ways. So this is your clients and your business. So if you decrease your price, you decrease your clients' emotional investment. If this was $99 a month, a lot of people wouldn't even be here. Here, right? You guys, or your clients. If you decrease the price of your membership, they don't value it as much. You literally have a bottle of wine that's $100, a bottle of wine that's $10, it tastes better. Like, proven a million times over. It tastes better. They literally value it more because it's more expensive. Third, because they value it, they don't value it, they don't get good results. And then because of that, they increase their demandiness, which is kind of one of the most ironic ones, is that who hears your person who's paying you $79 a month from your oldest membership is the pain in the ass. Right? Right? It's insane. And all your like, you're like, but I love my challenges. They pay $200 a month and they're like, so happy to be here. Like, this is great. We don't do bar balls. I don't care. I'm just happy to be here. You're like, why aren't we doing double unders and more? And you're like choking them with a jump rope. You're like, don't worry about it. It's fine. Don't worry, keep walking. But then the people become more demanding when you're at prices, right? And then your revenue for fulfillment for the customer decreases too. So if you have a $78 a month membership, for someone to come unlimited to your crossfit, you don't have any money left over to even do anything with, right? So like, it's this negative cycle that keeps happening. It's like, they get more demanding. You can do less. They have less emotional investment. They're not valuing it much. They're not getting good results because they don't value it much. And they're like, that place sucks. It's a piece of crap. And to be fair, they're probably right, right? Now your business, so that's just from your client's angle. So if you, raw thoughts. If you, like when you decrease your price, you decrease your profits, right? When you decrease your price, you decrease your perceived value of self. Like who here would love to have the only gym that could like do crossfit and do, I'm just using crossfit because a lot of you guys are. And charge $400 a month for crossfit. Wouldn't that make you feel cool? It'd be like, we're by far the most expensive, but we're also the best. And like, it's a cool feeling to say that. And I love saying that about Arbus. This is like, we are by far the most expensive. I was like, but we are also the best. And then you decrease your perception of impact. Because then when you have that, you keep, you start seeing testimonials, testimonials, testimonials, all these things. And you're like, I'm making an impact. And as an entrepreneur, that's what we feed off of from momentum. Like, I can't tell you how many times people came up even during the photos last night. Like, I know you get tired of hearing them. Like, nope. Keep them coming. Like, like, keep them coming. Totally fine. Cause like it helps, right? Cause you get disconnected and like, we never get overwhelmed with positive feedback. Like you're good. But if you decrease your price, you decrease the service level, right? That you're even to be able to ride. And then lastly, and this is a big one. If you're not delivering the results, right? This guy, and you're not seeing that on a regular basis, your sales team will lose conviction, right? And a lot of times your sales team is you for right now. So sometimes like who here has had a challenger blow them up right before a sales consultation and tell them that they're not losing weight. And they're like, what the heck? Three people, five people. All right, seven people. Okay. Well, I have had that happen. And it totally ruins your afternoon. So what's your goal? Let me show you the shortest tour of all time. You know, like you sit them down and you have this. And so obviously the flip side of this is the right way to do it, right? You increase your price, they're more invested. They perceive it higher. They get better results because they're perceiving the value is higher and they're more invested. They're less demanding because the price goes up. And now you have more money to then fulfill with them for. You also make more money, so you're happier. Your perceived value of self goes up because you're like, yeah, this is amazing. Perception of your impact goes up because you're like, wow, we're spending all this money delivering amazing service to these people. And then finally, your sales seems like, listen, you can go wherever you want, but you're gonna end up here either way. So you might as well just like get going now. Because we're gonna be the ones who fix it for good. That's what you wanna be able to have. So to wrap this all up, I'm doing it on time, look at me. You have to adopt a mindset of weaponry and compassion for your customers and your employees. Lately covered more of the employee side and hopefully I covered a little bit more of the customer side, but a problem save, sorry, sorry. Allergies. A problem solving massive action taking mindset of creating solutions to every problem that they have. Every problem, not just gym problems, every problem that they have. The more problems, the more areas in their life that you can positively affect. We had that marriage talk, we got a ton of good feedback on it, don't worry, we're not talking about that all the time. But it's good to have little things like that because it affects all areas of someone's life. But blaming yourself and never your customers or your employees for any negative outcome and giving your employees and your customers credit for every positive outcome. When your gym crushes it, be like, we did such a good, like team, we crushed it, you guys are so good, right? Versus like, we have a bad month, you're like, that's on me guys. I didn't clarify the goals well enough, like we didn't have enough this or that or whatever, like you have to take that and it's just a sign of a good leader. Because what this will allow you to do is it'll allow you to acquire a customer more profitably from a premium pricing standpoint and keep them longer because of lifetime value. And that's why I am such a pretty prompter on the whole green play. And then that's where you get into this beautiful place of abundance. So here's my final thoughts. Hopefully, thank you for staying with me. What this presentation is, how much can you serve, how much more can you serve, excuse me? So think about it and be real with yourself if you're like, like who here thinks they're doing it, like the best they possibly can with their customers. See now no one raised their hand. Just like every other time, okay. How many more problems could you solve that you haven't even asked yourself to solve yet? How can you blow people away and demonstrate your personal growth through business growth? Like I love it when people who haven't seen us in a while are like, man, you just seem like, like last time I saw you, like the intensity's still there, but they're like, it's way clearer now. I was like, thanks. So it's like they see the growth. Like you want your clients to see your growth and like what Layla was saying, you want to act in a way that would inspire loyalty, would inspire a following. Like I have people who like, so Christine, who you might have seen with the headset, she was my like 19th client at my first gym. And so six years later, she saw an ad and was like, Alex, I'll work for Alex, like Alex seem cool. You know what I mean? Like if you can inspire that kind of thing, you have great people. Our sales team, we have eight guys on the launch side. When we were doing the done for you side, when we were flying guys out, at one point when we did the transition to teaching people how to fish, instead of fishing for them, I had to let all of them go on the same day. So I was just like, so we had eight guys. I was like, this sucks. Because they're all good dudes, they left their jobs to do this. And, but what I did was I set every single one of them up with a job before I told them. So I had set, or if they didn't have a job, I set them up with three interviews. So that they would like, like, and they were promised, like, hey, if Alex, you had a cell, like I know you can close. And so months later, this is now like this month, we had, we posted our, you know, hey, we need another sales person. And all of them came back this month, because they're like, we didn't know that you had openings. I heard Derek got hired again. I was like, yeah, yeah, Derek's a closer for sure. And Danny was right by his like, oh, Derek's in? It's like, can I get my, like, can I work? I was like, oh yeah, you can work. And those guys were absolute hustlers. Cause like, I told those guys under my wing, like my own, like those guys are just like you, you know, I mean, like the kids crying on the floor and you're trying to get a credit card number, or like toe to toe, face to face, in a different city every single month. But the only reason they came back is because we treated them well. And it was something that they were like, we just want to be a part of this. We see how much you guys have grown. And if you can create that in your community, people will start coming to you, to start attracting those types of people. Okay, so Layla and I will be moving forward with developing these things out for you so that you can better serve your customers, which are kind of covered, which is kind of a win-win.
